operating_state: Total revenue increased 5% to $10.6 billion in 2025 driven by property segment growth and services expansion. International operations delivered broad-based growth with double-digit increases in Europe and Africa & APAC, offset by Latin America declining due to foreign exchange headwinds and increased revenue reserves. The company added approximately 2,230 communications sites globally and maintains over $54 billion in non-cancellable tenant lease revenue with 59% of revenues concentrated among four telecommunications customers.
cost_structure: Property segment gross margin increased 4% to $7.7 billion with margin expansion in most regions except Latin America. Operating expenses increased modestly with personnel costs and canceled construction expenses partially offset by decreased stock-based compensation. Data Centers segment gross margin grew 22% benefiting from a property tax settlement, while services gross margin compressed as revenue expansion outpaced cost optimization.
material_events: Completed $3.0 billion senior notes issuance while repaying $3.2 billion of maturing debt. Sold South Africa Fiber business generating $138 million proceeds and $54 million gain. Repurchased $365 million of common stock and paid $3.1 billion in dividends. Acquired 312 communications sites. Eugene Reilly elected to Board while Olivier Puech announced retirement as President, International effective January 2026.
macro_exposures: Foreign currency translation losses totaled $809 million in 2025 compared to $308 million gains in 2024, primarily from intercompany note exposures with 31% of revenues denominated in foreign currencies. AT&T Mexico dispute resulted in reserves with customer withholding approximately $300 million in annual tower rents pending arbitration. Rising interest rates create customer liquidity risks and may constrain carrier capital expenditure budgets.
forward_outlook: 2026 capital expenditures projected at $1.8-1.9 billion including approximately 1,700-2,300 new communications sites and data center assets. Management expects continued reserves related to AT&T Mexico arbitration until settlement. Data center segment positioned for AI workload growth through hybrid-cloud deployments with purpose-built capacity for higher-density requirements. Company maintains $9.6 billion available borrowing capacity under credit facilities.
uncertainty_profile: AT&T Mexico arbitration outcome remains uncertain with potential material negative impact until August 2026 resolution. DISH litigation regarding Strategic Collocation Agreement obligations is pending. Customer concentration risk persists creating sensitivity to creditworthiness changes. Cybersecurity threats have increased potentially exposing the company to operational disruptions and regulatory penalties. Data center developments face power grid constraints that could restrict expansion capabilities.
